segments.title: Bruships 245 waits for the Discord questions-open signal
segments.summary: The transcript shows the host waiting for the Discord questions-open signal before changing the live banner.
segments.body: This portion sets up the live Q&A format. It marks where the live Q&A system becomes available, while leaving the actual alternate-history and naval-history answers for a

evidence.note: Transcript references the Discord questions-open signal and banner change.

segments.title: Washington Naval Treaty without treaty battleships
segments.summary: A question asks about a Washington Naval Treaty scenario without the treaty battleships.
segments.body: The answer explores why changing treaty battleship outcomes creates large downstream consequences.

evidence.note: A question asks about a Washington Naval Treaty scenario without the treaty battleships.

segments.question: If a Washington Naval Treaty scenario avoids the treaty battleships, why is that a bad or difficult path?
segments.answerShort: Because removing those ships changes tonnage, design incentives, and later fleet balance in ways that are hard to contain.

segments.title: Less punitive Versailles alternate history
segments.summary: The stream considers a version of Versailles that is less punitive if blame and war outcomes shift.
segments.body: The answer treats altered responsibility and treaty terms as producing a substantially different interwar path.

evidence.note: The stream considers a version of Versailles that is less punitive if blame and war outcomes shift.

segments.question: What happens if the Central Powers are not blamed the same way and Versailles is less punitive?
segments.answerShort: A less punitive settlement likely changes interwar politics and naval conditions, but the whole treaty environment shifts.

segments.title: Warspite changes question
segments.summary: A question asks what changes would be made to Warspite in an alternate path.
segments.body: The answer uses the refit context to think through modernization choices and constraints.

evidence.note: A question asks what changes would be made to Warspite in an alternate path.

segments.question: What changes would be made to Warspite in this alternate scenario?
segments.answerShort: The answer treats Warspite modernization as a constrained refit problem shaped by timing, ship condition, and fleet needs.

segments.title: Coal-fired ship options question
segments.summary: A question asks what coal-fired ships could be used and how coal quality affects options.
segments.body: The answer discusses coal-powered cruisers and the difference between high- and lower-quality coal.

evidence.note: A question asks what coal-fired ships could be used and how coal quality affects options.

segments.question: What coal-fired ships could be used, and how does coal quality affect the choice?
segments.answerShort: Coal quality matters; some coal-powered cruisers are more plausible than others depending on available coal and machinery tolerance.

segments.title: Attacking carriers in harbor question
segments.summary: A Q&A window considers carriers in harbor and whether smaller carriers change fleet-carrier development.
segments.body: The answer distinguishes cruiser carriers and smaller carriers from the fleet-carrier route.

evidence.note: A Q&A window considers carriers in harbor and whether smaller carriers change fleet-carrier development.

segments.question: What happens if carriers are caught in harbor, and should 10,000-ton cruiser carriers affect fleet carriers?
segments.answerShort: Smaller carriers may evolve separately, but the fleet-carrier route still has its own logic and levels of capability.

segments.title: Accessible coal and wood/charcoal question
segments.summary: A viewer asks about deeper coal seams and whether wood or charcoal remains viable.
segments.body: The answer treats fuel accessibility as a major constraint on naval and industrial alternatives.

evidence.note: A viewer asks about deeper coal seams and whether wood or charcoal remains viable.

segments.question: If accessible coal seams are deeper, are wood and charcoal viable instead?
segments.answerShort: Fuel constraints become serious; wood and charcoal cannot simply replace coal for industrial naval power at scale.

segments.title: Silliest naval ship idea question
segments.summary: A late question asks for the silliest naval ship idea.
segments.body: The discussion moves through extreme battleship and submarine aircraft carrier ideas as intentionally absurd design prompts.

evidence.note: A late question asks for the silliest naval ship idea.

segments.question: What is the silliest naval ship idea?
segments.answerShort: The answer plays with oversized battleships and submarine aircraft carriers, treating them as deliberately absurd design extremes.
